# Vault Index

## ActualChat / LibreChat

- [[actualchat-knowledge-map|ActualChat Knowledge Map]] — Guided overview of all ActualChat and LibreChat pages
- [[actualchat-iris|ActualChat (IRIS)]] — Actual Reality's fork of LibreChat, three repos, Railway deployment
- [[librechat|LibreChat]] — Upstream open-source AI chat platform (Express.js + React + MongoDB)
- [[librechat-architecture|LibreChat Architecture]] — Request flow, backend/frontend structure, database, module aliases
- [[librechat-workspace-packages|LibreChat Workspace Packages]] — npm workspaces: data-provider → data-schemas → api → client, build order
- [[librechat-agents-lib|@librechat-agents]] — LangGraph-based TypeScript agent orchestration library
- [[rag-api|rag_api]] — Python FastAPI RAG service with PGVector/MongoDB/Qdrant
- [[librechat-agents|LibreChat Agents]] — No-code Agent Builder, 9 capabilities, Agents API (beta), ACL sharing
- [[mcp-in-librechat|MCP in LibreChat]] — Model Context Protocol: 4 transports, auth methods, deferred tools
- [[rag-architecture-in-librechat|RAG Architecture in LibreChat]] — Document retrieval pipeline: chunking → embedding → vector search
- [[librechat-web-search|LibreChat Web Search]] — Three-component: search provider → scraper → reranker
- [[librechat-configuration-system|LibreChat Configuration System]] — Dual config: .env (secrets) + librechat.yaml (app behavior)
- [[librechat-auth-system|LibreChat Auth System]] — Email + social (5) + SSO (OIDC/SAML/LDAP), JWT, rate limiting
- [[librechat-deployment|LibreChat Deployment]] — Docker, Railway, Helm, Redis for horizontal scaling
- [[librechat-observability|LibreChat Observability]] — Langfuse tracing, structured logging, resumable streams
- [[actualchat-customizations|ActualChat Customizations]] — Additive-customizations pattern over LibreChat; links to CUSTOMIZATIONS.md + SYNC_RUNBOOK.md; hook canary; known limits

## GameRight

- [[gameright|GameRight]] — Data contextualization engine. Atlas = universal truth. Services: Atlas, Demeter, Themis, Hephaestus, Dionysus. Families + tournaments + corporate stakeholders.
- [[atlas-gameright|Atlas (GameRight)]] — Data layer: game metadata + attributes + ratings + tagging. Powered by three ingestion pipelines writing to shared Postgres (Neon).
- [[demeter-gameright|Demeter (GameRight)]] — Data enrichment service. Runs inside Atlas pipelines, invokes Gemini to produce Game Attributes per title.
- [[themis-gameright|Themis (GameRight)]] — User management: relationships, permissions, RBAC, organizational structure. Currently realized via BetterAuth in NorthStar.
- [[hephaestus-gameright|Hephaestus (GameRight)]] — User tools: Dashboard, Calendar, multi-platform Game Library. Presentation layer across modules.
- [[dionysus-gameright|Dionysus (GameRight)]] — Activities and Events service. Template vs instance split with CPA-verified participation metrics.
- [[gameright-northstar|GameRight NorthStar]] — Premium family module. Next.js 16 + React 19 + Bun + Drizzle + Neon + BullMQ. Consumes Atlas API. MVP Phase 1/Phase 2 scoped.
- [[gameright-demo|GameRight Demo]] — Vite + React prototype w/ mocked data. Cloudflare Wrangler deploy target. Visual showcase for NorthStar vision.
- [[atlas-steam-pipeline|Atlas Steam Pipeline]] — Dagster, 6 assets. Steam API + Reviews + SteamSpy + IGDB → JSONL → Gemini → Postgres. Incremental.
- [[atlas-screenscraper-pipeline|Atlas ScreenScraper Pipeline]] — Dagster, 4 assets. Retro games from screenscraper.fr. 8 enabled platforms. Auto-links ports + regional variants.
- [[atlas-attribute-loader|Atlas Attribute Loader]] — Standalone seeder. init.sql + seed.sql + load_data.py. ~65K human-scored CSV rows.
- [[atlas-go-api|Atlas Go API]] — Echo v4 + Ent ORM. Read-only Postgres. X-API-Key auth. Swagger UI. 100 rps/key.
- [[game-attributes-gameright|Game Attributes (GameRight)]] — Characteristics (cozy/comp), Traits, Qualities (social/scholastic/interactive/meta), Age Rating 1–4. Full scoring rubric.
- [[atlas-loop-types|Atlas Loop Types]] — 25-category taxonomy of repeatable gameplay patterns. Drives activity matching and educational contextualization.
- [[atlas-composite-game-identity|Atlas Composite Game Identity]] — (external_id, source, platform, region) + `game_links`. Same title across platforms = separate records.
- [[atlas-database-schema|Atlas Database Schema]] — games + 17 attrs + traits + devs + publishers + age_ratings + links. TSVECTOR full-text search.
- [[gemini-scoring-system-gameright|Gemini Scoring System (GameRight)]] — gemini-3-pro-preview, 4 parallel category calls per game, 22 RPM, 5 few-shot examples per category.
- [[atlas-api-gameright|Atlas API (GameRight)]] — Cross-repo REST contract: /api/v1 + X-API-Key + paginated {total,page,per_page,results} + {error:{code,message}}.
- [[activities-vs-events-gameright|Activities vs Events (GameRight)]] — One Game → Many Activities → Many Events. Template static, instance live with CPA metrics.

## Jinn

- [[jinn|Jinn]] — Agentic UGC video production system for commerce brands; Lazarus orchestrates intake → DNA → strategy → ideation → scripts → compliance → characters → video
- [[lazarus-jinn|Lazarus (Jinn)]] — Master orchestrator workflow; webhook + Airtable intake; fans into 4 parallel intelligence passes, strategy, creative, compliance, character, video
- [[brand-dna-jinn|Brand DNA (Jinn)]] — Brand Constitution from Brand Analyzer - LAZ: identity, tribes, tone rules, sonic profile, positioning wedge
- [[product-dna-jinn|Product DNA (Jinn)]] — Product Fact Base from Product Page Analzyer - LAZ: specs, offer, benefit stack, mechanism, compliance risks, social proof
- [[brand-memory-capsule-jinn|Brand Memory Capsule (Jinn)]] — Central context bundle: brand_dna + product_dna + patterns + competitive intel + messaging architecture
- [[strategic-pillar-sub-jinn|Strategic Pillar Sub (Jinn)]] — Research-to-creative bridge; produces messaging architecture, pillars, tribe mappings; writes back to memory capsule
- [[jinn-pattern-intelligence|Jinn Pattern Intelligence]] — TT Video Scrapper + TT ViralVid Analys w/ Transcript → structured creative patterns from comments/transcript/performance
- [[jinn-ideation-and-hooks|Jinn Ideation + Hooks]] — Ideation Brief Generator (3 briefs) + Hook Generator (ranked phone-native hooks)
- [[jinn-script-router|Jinn Script Router]] — Orchestration layer: picks single best specialized writer (UGC testimonial, PAS, ASMR, storytime, founder, trend hijack, etc.)
- [[jinn-compliance-loop|Jinn Compliance Loop]] — Multi-agent gate: auditor (FDA/FTC) + guardrails + voice QA + CCO → GREEN_LIGHT/WARNING/LOCKED; preserves loop state
- [[jinn-character-system|Jinn Character System]] — Roster + 3-prompt bible (Foundation / Psychology+Voice / Movement+Master) + reference image generation
- [[jinn-video-pipeline|Jinn Video Pipeline]] — Stage 1 storyboard + hero/remaining frames + Veo package → Stage 2 Veo 3.1 Pro first-frame animation + QA
- [[jinn-system-prose-brief|jinn-system-prose-brief]] — Source summary of narrative Jinn brief
- [[jinn-system-structured-brief|jinn-system-structured-brief]] — Source summary of structured Jinn update with JSON workflow file refs

## Projects

- [[pathways-ai-semantic-layer|Pathways AI Semantic Layer]] — Translation layer between natural language and VQI PVI clinical registry schema (Oracle 26ai, Cohere, FastAPI)
- [[hypothesis-resolver-three-step-design|HypothesisResolver Three-Step Design]] — Resolution Mode pipeline: endpoint match → exposure ID → composition. Emits structured analytical context.
- [[pathways-ai-query-executor-gap|Pathways AI Query Executor Gap]] — Missing layer between HypothesisResolver context and actual patient-data computation. Blocks May 29 demo query.
- [[pathways-ai-frontend-semantic-layer-disconnect|Pathways AI Frontend Semantic Layer Disconnect]] — Explorer live, Hypothesis module on fixtures. Unification blocked on executor delivery.
- [[sourab-neo4j-implementation|Sourab's Neo4j Semantic Layer]] — Parallel implementation with 3-step ontology enrichment (RxNorm + SNOMED → Claude → Cohere)
- [[batting-cleanup|Batting Cleanup]] — Toledo smart-city QR reporting system; Deno monorepo on Cloudflare Workers/Pages (Hono + Postgres/PostGIS + React 19 admin)
- [[batting-cleanup-architecture|Batting Cleanup Architecture]] — Layered Handler → Service → Repo/Gateway with lazy service-container singletons for CF Workers
- [[batting-cleanup-database-schema|Batting Cleanup Database Schema]] — Postgres + PostGIS (SRID 4326) tables: owners, assets, reports, location logs, auth tokens, sessions, email logs
- [[batting-cleanup-admin-panel|Batting Cleanup Admin Panel]] — React 19 + TanStack (Router/Query/Table) + MapLibre SPA; magic-link auth; served by api worker via static-assets binding
- [[batting-cleanup-email-digest|Batting Cleanup Email Digest]] — Daily 9 AM EST CF cron: per-owner + global Resend digests, minute-granular idempotency keys, Neon warmup retry
- [[crockford-base32-asset-ids|Crockford Base32 Asset IDs]] — 6-char human-transcription-safe IDs (excludes I/L/O/U); ~1B namespace; DB unique constraint for collision defense

## Domain Knowledge

- [[clinical-registry-graph-composition|Clinical Registry Graph Composition]] — Four node types (fields, concepts, endpoints, cross-form links) and structural complexities (hierarchies, laterality, skip logic)
- [[semantic-layer-dual-mode-operation|Semantic Layer Dual-Mode Operation]] — Query mode (human-facing field search) vs Resolution mode (machine-facing hypothesis → analytical context)
- [[obt-data-shape|OBT Data Shape (PVI Registry)]] — One Big Table: ~922 columns, ~500K procedures, key identifiers, follow-up relationships

## Data Modeling

- [[kimball-dimensional-modeling|Kimball Dimensional Modeling]] — Overview of ~80 techniques from Kimball Group (star schema, facts, dimensions)
- [[kimball-group|Kimball Group]] — Ralph Kimball & Margy Ross, originators of dimensional modeling methodology
- [[kimball-fundamental-concepts|Kimball Fundamental Concepts]] — Four-step design process, grain, facts, dimensions, star schema
- [[kimball-fact-table-techniques|Kimball Fact Table Techniques]] — 6 basic + 13 advanced fact table patterns (transaction, snapshot, factless, etc.)
- [[kimball-dimension-table-techniques|Kimball Dimension Table Techniques]] — 12 basic + 14 advanced dimension patterns (surrogate keys, junk, role-playing, etc.)
- [[slowly-changing-dimensions|Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)]] — Types 0–7: retain, overwrite, add row, mini-dimension, dual
- [[kimball-integration-techniques|Kimball Integration Techniques]] — Conformed dims/facts, bus architecture, bus matrix, drilling across
- [[kimball-hierarchy-techniques|Kimball Hierarchy Techniques]] — Fixed depth, slightly ragged, ragged (bridge tables, pathstring)
- [[kimball-special-purpose-schemas|Kimball Special Purpose Schemas]] — Supertype/subtype, real-time dual-table, error event schemas
- [[kimball-dm-techniques-reference|Kimball DM Techniques Reference (2013)]] — Source summary of official Kimball Group PDF
- [[kimball-dw-bi-architecture|Kimball DW/BI Architecture]] — Four-component architecture: source → ETL → presentation → BI, with restaurant metaphor
- [[kimball-healthcare-dimensional-modeling|Healthcare Dimensional Modeling]] — Claims billing, multivalued diagnoses, measurement type dimension, retroactive changes
- [[kimball-modeling-mistakes|Kimball Top 10 Dimensional Modeling Mistakes]] — Anti-patterns: mixed grain, snowflaking hierarchies, operational keys, non-conformed dimensions
- [[kimball-etl-34-subsystems|Kimball ETL 34 Subsystems]] — Extract → Clean/Conform → Deliver → Manage framework for data warehouse ETL

## Game Design

- [[searchless|Searchless]] — Word-mode puzzle game; player trapped in a fake Google. Hub + 4 sub-sites, Mod antagonist, 5-cast recognition. Midwest Arcadia.
- [[searchless-keyword-discoverability|Searchless Keyword Discoverability]] — PAA-as-landmark pattern for surfacing next-keyword without breaking diegetic illusion
- [[project-bureau|Project: Bureau]] — Analog horror deduction game, Case Officer guiding agents through paranormal encounters (Midwest Arcadia, Toledo Codes Apr 2026)
- [[game-analysis-frameworks|Game Analysis Frameworks]] — Overview of MDA, Formal/Dramatic/Dynamic, and Elemental Tetrad
- [[mda-framework|MDA Framework]] — Hunicke/LeBlanc/Zubek: Mechanics → Dynamics → Aesthetics (2004)
- [[robin-hunicke|Robin Hunicke]] — MDA co-author, game designer and researcher
- [[marc-leblanc|Marc LeBlanc]] — MDA co-author, defined eight "kinds of fun"
- [[robert-zubek|Robert Zubek]] — MDA co-author, game designer and researcher
- [[formal-dramatic-dynamic-elements|Formal, Dramatic, and Dynamic Elements]] — Fullerton: 7 formal + 3 dramatic + dynamic elements
- [[elemental-tetrad|Elemental Tetrad]] — Schell's four-element diamond: Mechanics, Aesthetics, Story, Technology
- [[magic-circle|Magic Circle]] — Huizinga's game/reality boundary concept from *Homo Ludens* (1955)
- [[layered-tetrad|The Layered Tetrad]] — 4 elements x 3 layers game analysis framework (Gibson Bond, extends Schell)
- [[johan-huizinga|Johan Huizinga]] — Dutch historian, *Homo Ludens*, Magic Circle concept
- [[jesse-schell|Jesse Schell]] — Game designer, originator of Elemental Tetrad
- [[jeremy-gibson-bond|Jeremy Gibson Bond]] — Author of IGDPD, creator of Layered Tetrad
- [[layered-tetrad-source-summary|Layered Tetrad Source Summary]] — Full digest of IGDPD Chapter 3 lecture slides
- [[igdpd-ch02-game-analysis-frameworks|IGDPD Ch02 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 2 lecture slides
- [[definition-of-game|Definition of Game]] — Five competing definitions (Suits, Meier, Fullerton, Schell, Burgun) from IGDPD Ch.1
- [[iterative-game-design|Iterative Game Design]] — Play-analyze-modify-retest loop, single-variable changes, playtesting flukes
- [[igdpd|IGDPD]] — Textbook by Jeremy Gibson Bond (Pearson): three-layer framework, prototyping-first pedagogy
- [[inscribed-layer|Inscribed Layer]] — Everything designers fix into a game: mechanics, aesthetics, narrative, technology
- [[inscribed-mechanics|Inscribed Mechanics]] — Rules (objectives, restrictions, procedures, guidelines) and tables (probability, progression, playtest)
- [[inscribed-aesthetics|Inscribed Aesthetics]] — Sensory elements: vision, hearing (SFX/music/dialogue), touch, scent, taste
- [[inscribed-narrative|Inscribed Narrative]] — Premise, setting, character, plot. Freytag 5-act and Syd Field 3-act structures
- [[interactive-vs-linear-narrative|Interactive vs Linear Narrative]] — Plot vs free will, empathetic character vs avatar, limited agency methods
- [[inscribed-narrative-purposes|Inscribed Narrative Purposes]] — Evoking emotion, motivation/justification, progression/reward, mechanics reinforcement
- [[inscribed-technology|Inscribed Technology]] — Paper (dice, state tracking, tech trees) and digital (platforms, engines)
- [[dynamic-layer|Dynamic Layer]] — Second layer of Layered Tetrad: everything during play, shared dev/player ownership
- [[dynamic-mechanics|Dynamic Mechanics]] — House rules, Bartle player types, four outcome layers (immediate/quest/cumulative/final)
- [[bartle-player-types|Bartle Player Types]] — Achiever/Explorer/Socializer/Killer taxonomy from MUD research (2x2 grid)
- [[john-bartle|John Bartle]] — Creator of Bartle player types taxonomy from MUD research
- [[dynamic-aesthetics|Dynamic Aesthetics]] — Procedural (music, particles, animation) + environmental (play context, accessibility)
- [[procedural-music|Procedural Music]] — Dynamically generated/adapted music during gameplay
- [[dynamic-narrative|Dynamic Narrative]] — Interactive fiction (adaptive story) + emergent narrative (player's own story)
- [[janet-murray|Janet Murray]] — Author of *Hamlet on the Holodeck*, interactive fiction theorist
- [[dynamic-technology|Dynamic Technology]] — Runtime behavior of paper (dice variance) and digital (code execution, AI, OS)
- [[igdpd-ch05-dynamic-layer|IGDPD Ch05 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 5 lecture slides (The Dynamic Layer)
- [[cultural-layer|Cultural Layer]] — Outermost Layered Tetrad layer: game beyond play, player/society agency
- [[cultural-mechanics|Cultural Mechanics]] — Player ownership of mechanics: mods, custom levels, behavioral bleed
- [[cultural-aesthetics|Cultural Aesthetics]] — Community art: game art, fan art, cosplay, gameplay as performance
- [[cultural-narrative|Cultural Narrative]] — Player stories: fan fiction, narrative mods, machinima, media narratives
- [[cultural-technology|Cultural Technology]] — Game tech beyond games, player-made tools
- [[cultural-impact|Cultural Impact]] — Positive/negative effects, First Amendment, designer ethics
- [[igdpd-ch06-the-cultural-layer|IGDPD Ch06 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 6 lecture slides (The Cultural Layer)
- [[iterative-design-spiral|Iterative Design Spiral]] — Four-quadrant spiral: Analysis → Design → Implementation → Testing (Ch.7)
- [[design-attitude-of-listening|Design Attitude of Listening]] — Five listening dimensions: audience, team, client, game, yourself
- [[playtesting-methodology|Playtesting Methodology]] — Structured feedback notes, Schell's framing, severity triage
- [[incremental-vs-intersectional-innovation|Incremental vs Intersectional Innovation]] — Johansson's two innovation types from The Medici Effect
- [[paper-prototyping|Paper Prototyping]] — Low-fidelity prototypes as fastest path to testing
- [[the-medici-effect|The Medici Effect]] — Johansson's book on innovation at the intersection of disciplines
- [[frans-johansson|Frans Johansson]] — Author of The Medici Effect
- [[igdpd-ch07-acting-like-a-designer|IGDPD Ch07 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 7 lecture slides
- [[design-goals|Design Goals]] — Designer-centric (fortune, fame, community, expression, greater good, craft) and player-centric (fun, flow, empowerment, decisions)
- [[caillois-four-types-of-play|Caillois Four Types of Play]] — Agon (competitive), Alea (chance), Ilinx (vertigo), Mimicry (make-believe)
- [[igdpd-ch08-design-goals|IGDPD Ch08 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 8 lecture slides
- [[player-guidance|Player Guidance]] — Direct (tutorials, HUD) vs indirect (subtle behavioral shaping)
- [[seven-methods-of-indirect-guidance|Seven Methods of Indirect Guidance]] — Constraints, Goals, Physical Interface, Visual Design, Audio Design, Player Avatar, NPCs
- [[igdpd-ch13-guiding-the-player|IGDPD Ch13 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 13 lecture slides
- [[puzzle-design|Puzzle Design]] — Kim's definition, modes of thought (Word/Image/Logic), 8 design steps, 7 goals
- [[scott-kim|Scott Kim]] — Puzzle designer (Discover, Pogo.com), originator of puzzle design framework in IGDPD
- [[kyle-gabler|Kyle Gabler]] — Co-creator of World of Goo, "juicy" feedback concept
- [[igdpd-ch12-puzzles|IGDPD Ch12 Source Summary]] — Digest of Chapter 12 lecture slides (Puzzles)

## Developer Tools

- [[entire|Entire]] — Developer platform capturing AI agent sessions as versioned Git data (MIT, local-first)
- [[checkpoints-entire|Checkpoints (Entire)]] — Save-point snapshots linked to commits via trailers, temporary + committed types
- [[sessions-entire|Sessions (Entire)]] — Full agent interaction capture: transcripts, file changes, token metrics, nested sessions
- [[line-attribution-entire|Line Attribution (Entire)]] — Agent vs human contribution tracking per commit (percentage + line counts)
- [[entire-shadow-branches|Entire Shadow Branches]] — Temporary mid-session branches for rewind, auto-cleaned, never pushed
- [[entire-docs-source-summary|Entire Docs (Introduction + Core Concepts)]] — Source digest of docs.entire.io (intro, core concepts, quickstart)

## Comparisons

- [[oracle-vs-neo4j-semantic-layer|Oracle vs Neo4j — Semantic Layer]] — Trade-off analysis: SQL integration vs ontology enrichment, OCI compatibility, pending client decision

## Spaceport

- [[spaceport-framework|Spaceport Framework]] — Groovy/JVM fullstack framework. CouchDB built in. Alerts, Launchpad `.ghtml`, server-side reactive templating. Hot-reload.
- [[create-spaceport-app|create-spaceport-app]] — Bootstrap kit. Paste `BOOTSTRAP.md` into Claude Code, answer 6 questions, get scaffold + 6 agents + living docs.
- [[spaceport-scaffold-levels|Spaceport Scaffold Levels]] — port-bouey (backend-only), port-voyager (public + 1 auth), port-enterprise (marketing+SaaS+admin), custom.
- [[spaceport-claude-agents|Spaceport Claude Agents]] — Six subagents: Consultant, Routing, Launchpad, Data Modeling (advisory); CouchDB Probe, Dev Probe (operational).

## Claude Code Internals

- [[claude-code-unpacked|Claude Code Unpacked]] — Unofficial source analysis of Claude Code (ccunpacked.dev, by @razakiau)
- [[claude-code-agent-loop|Claude Code Agent Loop]] — 11-step pipeline: input → system prompt → API streaming → tool loop → render
- [[claude-code-tool-system|Claude Code Tool System]] — 53+ tools in 8 categories (file ops, execution, agents, MCP, etc.)
- [[claude-code-command-catalog|Claude Code Command Catalog]] — 95+ slash commands in 5 categories (setup, workflow, git, debug, experimental)
- [[claude-code-source-architecture|Claude Code Source Architecture]] — Directory treemap: 1,900+ files, 30+ dirs under src/
- [[claude-code-hidden-features|Claude Code Hidden Features]] — 8 unreleased: Buddy, Kairos, UltraPlan, Coordinator, Bridge, Daemon, UDS Inbox, Auto-Dream

## AI Models & Research

- [[muse-spark|Muse Spark]] — Meta's first Muse-family model: natively multimodal, 10x compute reduction vs Llama 4, 58% HLE
- [[meta-superintelligence-labs|Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL)]] — Meta's lab building "personal superintelligence," Muse model family
- [[thought-compression|Thought Compression]] — Test-time reasoning: extend then compress reasoning tokens for efficiency
- [[multi-agent-test-time-reasoning|Multi-Agent Test-Time Reasoning]] — Parallel agent reasoning at inference, comparable quality at lower latency
- [[muse-spark-announcement|Muse Spark Announcement (Apr 2026)]] — Source summary of Meta blog post announcing Muse Spark
- [[turboquant|TurboQuant]] — Google's two-stage vector compression: 3-bit KV cache, zero accuracy loss, 8x speedup (ICLR 2026)
- [[polarquant|PolarQuant]] — Stage 1: polar coordinate quantization, eliminates normalization overhead (AISTATS 2026)
- [[quantized-johnson-lindenstrauss|Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss (QJL)]] — Stage 2: 1-bit residual correction via JL transform sign bits
- [[kv-cache-quantization|KV Cache Quantization]] — Compressing transformer KV caches for inference efficiency
- [[turboquant-announcement|TurboQuant Announcement (Mar 2026)]] — Source summary of Google Research blog post

## AI Infrastructure Patterns

- [[virtual-filesystem-for-ai-agents|Virtual Filesystem for AI Agents]] — Intercept UNIX commands, translate to DB queries instead of container sandboxes
- [[chromafs|ChromaFs]] — Mintlify's virtual filesystem over ChromaDB (100ms boot, ~$0 marginal cost)
- [[mintlify|Mintlify]] — Docs platform company, built ChromaFs for AI assistant at 850K conversations/month
- [[coarse-then-fine-search|Coarse-then-Fine Search]] — Two-stage: DB as broad filter, in-memory regex for precision
- [[chunk-reassembly-pattern|Chunk Reassembly Pattern]] — Reconstruct full documents from vector store chunks via chunk_index sort + join
- [[pageindex|PageIndex]] — Vectify AI's vectorless RAG: JSON ToC tree as in-context index, LLM navigates iteratively
- [[pageindex-ocr|PageIndex OCR]] — Long-context VLM-based OCR; produces page markdown + ToC tree consumed by the PageIndex RAG framework
- [[reasoning-based-retrieval|Reasoning-Based Retrieval]] — Pattern: replace top-K similarity with iterative LLM navigation over a structured index
- [[vector-rag-limitations|Vector RAG Limitations]] — Five named failure modes of top-K cosine retrieval (mismatch, similarity≠relevance, chunking, no chat history, in-doc references)
- [[context-blindness|Context Blindness]] — Stateful reasoner / stateless retriever mismatch; the structural cause underlying all five vector RAG limitations
- [[pageindex-ocr-blog-2025-08|PageIndex OCR Blog (Aug 2025)]] — Source summary: PageIndex OCR product announcement; first long-context OCR model
- [[pageindex-blog-2025-09|PageIndex Blog (Sep 2025)]] — Source summary of the PageIndex intro blog post
- [[pageindex-cookbooks|PageIndex Cookbooks]] — SDK surface, canonical retrieval prompt, text + vision pipelines from the GitHub cookbook
- [[vision-rag|Vision RAG]] — Skip OCR; retrieve pages and feed images directly to a VLM (GPT-4.1, Qwen-VL, DeepSeek-OCR)
- [[do-we-need-ocr-blog-2025-10|Do We Still Need OCR? Blog (Oct 2025)]] — Information-theoretic case for vision-based RAG; nuanced view on when OCR remains useful
- [[pageindex-technical-manuals-blog-2025-12|PageIndex Technical Manuals Blog (Dec 2025)]] — Empirical comparison: PageIndex Chat vs. Gemini File Search RAG on industrial manuals
- [[pageindex-context-blindness-blog-2026-02|PageIndex Context Blindness Blog (Feb 2026)]] — Reframes retrieval as relevance classification; AlphaGo-style hierarchical tree search
- [[context-rot|Context Rot]] — LLM/VLM accuracy decays with sequence length; the underlying motivation for retrieval as a discipline

## Dev Practices

- [[git-filter-repo-workflow|git-filter-repo Workflow]] — destructive history rewrite. Reverts `.gitignore` mid-rebase; needs follow-up commit. Detaches remote to prevent accidental push.

## Operational Patterns

- [[oracle-railway-volume-fix|Oracle Railway Volume Fix]] — Entrypoint wrapper to fix UID 54321 volume permissions on Railway
- [[json-dump-db-provisioning|JSON Dump DB Provisioning]] — Export local Oracle → JSON fixtures → auto-import on remote boot without public DB exposure

    feeds a legal takedown, the cloaking diff + timestamped WARC is your
    evidence.


    **5. Hunt for siblings.** `careers-nike` is a pattern. Once we have the
    exfil endpoint/Telegram token, pivot on it (urlscan/VT search, `pages.dev`
    enumeration) — these kits get redeployed across dozens of subdomains, and
    reporting the channel kills them all at once.


    Want me to start with **#1 (deobfuscate and extract the exfil endpoint)**?
    That's the piece that makes everything else more effective. I'll do it as
    static string-array decoding — no running the kit's code.

    ingestion, and whether seeding has run.

    The architecture is clear: jobs aren't seeded manually — they're ingested by
    a cron job (`/api/cron/ingest-jobs`) that polls Slack `#job-share`, parses
    messages with an LLM, and upserts. "Seeding the DB" here means **running
    that ingest at least once**. Let me check the current state before
    triggering anything.

    current job count and the ingest cursor — plus the Slack gateway's fetch
    window.

    exist, how many jobs are in it, and what's the cursor state. I'll write a

<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

## appliedLabsPlane

## Epics

There are no epic tools — an epic is a work item whose type is named "Epic". Work
items always belong to a project; ask which if one is not named.
1. type = resolve_work_item_type(project_id, "Epic") — type.id is the type_id.
2. Create: create_work_item(project_id, type_id=type.id, name=...).
3. List: list_work_items(project_id, pql='type = "<type id>"').
4. Read / update / delete / nest: retrieve_work_item / update_work_item /
   delete_work_item by work item id (set parent=<work item id> to nest).
5. List an epic's children: list_work_items(project_id, pql='childOf("<EPIC-IDENTIFIER>")')
   using the epic's human-readable identifier (e.g. "PROJ-12") from retrieve_work_item.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## ojtech-plane

## Epics

There are no epic tools — an epic is a work item whose type is named "Epic". Work
items always belong to a project; ask which if one is not named.
1. type = resolve_work_item_type(project_id, "Epic") — type.id is the type_id.
2. Create: create_work_item(project_id, type_id=type.id, name=...).
3. List: list_work_items(project_id, pql='type = "<type id>"').
4. Read / update / delete / nest: retrieve_work_item / update_work_item /
   delete_work_item by work item id (set parent=<work item id> to nest).
5. List an epic's children: list_work_items(project_id, pql='childOf("<EPIC-IDENTIFIER>")')
   using the epic's human-readable identifier (e.g. "PROJ-12") from retrieve_work_item.

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Always call get_editor_state(include_schema: true) if you don't have the current .pen file schema in the context. Knowing the schema is required to use any other Pencil MCP tool.

Tools: get_editor_state, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, export_nodes.

## posthog
Below are tools available in the MCP. Prioritize skills over tools.

attachment.content:
- cora-email: Use when the user asks about their email, inbox, briefs, email todos, or wants to interact with Cora — an AI email assistant. Covers checking briefs, managing todos, drafting replies, and chatting with Cora via the CLI.
- impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- plane-time-tracking: Automatically tracks Claude Code session time to Plane project management issues. This skill MUST be activated at the very start of every Claude Code session — before doing any other work. It reads a `.plane` config from ~/.claude/.plane, parses the current git branch to extract the issue identifier (e.g., PROJ-123), starts a timer via the Plane API, and stops it when the session ends. If the branch doesn't follow the naming convention or the user is on main/master, the skill asks the user which issue to track to. Use this skill whenever: a session begins in a repo with a git branch, the user mentions time tracking, the user says "start timer" or "stop timer", or the user asks about logging work to Plane.
- plus-one-ssh: Use when the user asks to inspect, update, fix, or customize a Plus One bot over SSH.
- react-nextjs: React 19 + Next.js 15 code patterns and examples for TypeScript. Covers Server/Client Components, App Router conventions, React 19 features (use hook, actions, useOptimistic, compiler), TanStack Query, form handling with Zod, Zustand, error boundaries, and performance patterns. Use when building React components, pages, or Next.js features. Complements CLAUDE.md — does not repeat architecture, file structure, or TypeScript style rules already defined there.
- use-spark: Use the spark CLI to access the user's Spark email data - list emails, search by topic, read threads, check calendar events, find availability, look up contacts, and view team info. Use when the user asks about their emails, calendar, contacts, meetings, or scheduling.
- start-timer: Start Time Tracking
- stop-timer: Stop Time Tracking
- tracking-status: Time Tracking Status
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- kb:extract: Extract wiki-worthy knowledge from unprocessed session manifests
- kb:ingest: Ingest a source (file, URL, or pasted text) into the KB vault
- kb:lint: Lint the KB vault — orphans, dead links, stale content, contradictions
- kb:query: Query the KB vault and synthesize an answer from wiki pages
- kb:refine: Refine the KB vault — stale pages, weak connections, merges, backlinks
- compound-engineering:ce-agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue, then write a right-sized requirements document. Use when the user says "let's brainstorm", "what should we build", or "help me think through X", presents a vague or ambitious feature request, or seems unsure about scope or direction -- even without explicitly asking to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-clean-gone-branches: Clean up local branches whose remote tracking branch is gone. Use when the user says "clean up branches", "delete gone branches", "prune local branches", "clean gone", or wants to remove stale local branches that no longer exist on the remote. Also handles removing associated worktrees for branches that have them.
- compound-engineering:ce-code-review: Structured code review using tiered persona agents, confidence-gated findings, and a merge/dedup pipeline. Use when reviewing code changes before creating a PR.
- compound-engineering:ce-commit: Create a git commit with a clear, value-communicating message. Use when the user says "commit", "commit this", "save my changes", "create a commit", or wants to commit staged or unstaged work. Produces well-structured commit messages that follow repo conventions when they exist, and defaults to conventional commit format otherwise.
- compound-engineering:ce-commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR with an adaptive, value-first description that scales in depth with the change. Use when the user says "commit and PR", "ship this", "create a PR", or "open a pull request". Also handles description-only flows ("write a PR description", "rewrite the PR body", "describe this PR") without committing or pushing.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-compound-refresh: Refresh stale learning and pattern docs under docs/solutions/ by reviewing them against the current codebase, then updating, consolidating, or deleting drifted ones. Use when the user asks to "refresh my learnings", "audit docs/solutions/", "clean up stale learnings", or "consolidate overlapping docs", or when ce-compound flags an older doc as superseded. Do not trigger for general refactor, debugging, or code-review work unless the user has explicitly pointed at docs/solutions/.
- compound-engineering:ce-debug: Systematically find root causes and fix bugs. Use when debugging errors, investigating test failures, reproducing bugs from issue trackers (GitHub, Linear, Jira), or when stuck on a problem after failed fix attempts. Also use when the user says 'debug this', 'why is this failing', 'fix this bug', 'trace this error', or pastes stack traces, error messages, or issue references.
- compound-engineering:ce-demo-reel: Capture a visual demo reel (GIF, terminal recording, screenshots) for PR descriptions. Use when shipping UI changes, CLI features, or any work with observable behavior that benefits from visual proof. Also use when asked to add a demo, record a GIF, screenshot a feature, show what changed visually, create a demo reel, capture evidence, add proof to a PR, or create a before/after comparison.
- compound-engineering:ce-dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:ce-doc-review: Review requirements or plan documents using parallel persona agents that surface role-specific issues. Use when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:ce-frontend-design: Build web interfaces with genuine design quality, not AI slop. Use for any frontend work - landing pages, web apps, dashboards, admin panels, components, interactive experiences. Activates for both greenfield builds and modifications to existing applications. Detects existing design systems and respects them. Covers composition, typography, color, motion, and copy. Verifies results via screenshots before declaring done.
- compound-engineering:ce-gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded ideas about a topic. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising directions, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong options before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on X', 'surprise me', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-optimize: Run metric-driven iterative optimization loops -- define a measurable goal, run parallel experiments, measure each against hard gates or LLM-as-judge scores, keep improvements, and converge on the best solution. Use when optimizing clustering quality, search relevance, build performance, prompt quality, or any measurable outcome that benefits from systematic experimentation.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Create structured plans for multi-step tasks -- software features, research workflows, events, study plans, or any goal that benefits from breakdown. Also deepens existing plans with interactive sub-agent review. Use when the user says 'plan this', 'create a plan', 'how should we build', 'break this down', or when a brainstorm doc is ready for planning. Use 'deepen the plan' or 'deepening pass' for the deepening flow. For exploratory requests, prefer ce-brainstorm first.
- compound-engineering:ce-product-pulse: Generate a time-windowed pulse report on what users experienced and how the product performed - usage, quality, errors, signals worth investigating. Use when the user says 'run a pulse', 'show me the pulse', 'how are we doing', 'weekly recap', 'launch-day check', or passes a time window like '24h' or '7d'. Configures via .compound-engineering/config.local.yaml and saves reports to docs/pulse-reports/.
- compound-engineering:ce-proof: Run human-in-the-loop review loops over markdown via Proof (proofeditor.ai) — share, view, comment on, edit, and sync collaborative docs. Use when the user says "view this in proof", "share to proof", "HITL this doc", or wants a shared markdown review surface for a spec, plan, or draft, including handoffs from ce-brainstorm, ce-ideate, or ce-plan. Do not trigger on "proof" meaning evidence, math proofs, proof-of-concept, or "proofread this".
- compound-engineering:ce-resolve-pr-feedback: Resolve PR review feedback by evaluating validity and fixing issues in parallel. Use when addressing PR review comments, resolving review threads, or fixing code review feedback.
- compound-engineering:ce-riffrec-feedback-analysis: Riffrec product-feedback workflow. ALWAYS load when the user posts a `riffrec-*.zip`, a bundle with `session.json` + `events.json` + `recording.webm` + `voice.webm`, a video/audio recording for product feedback, or asks how to capture and share Riffrec sessions. Routes between setup, quick bug report, and extensive analysis.
- compound-engineering:ce-sessions: Search and ask questions about coding agent session history across Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor. Use when asking what was worked on, what was tried before, how a problem was investigated across sessions, what happened recently, or any question about past agent sessions. Also use when the user references prior sessions, previous attempts, or past investigations — even without saying 'sessions' explicitly.
- compound-engineering:ce-simplify-code: Simplify and refine recently changed code for clarity, reuse, quality, and efficiency while preserving behavior.
- compound-engineering:ce-slack-research: Search Slack for interpreted organizational context -- decisions, constraints, and discussion arcs -- and produce a synthesized research digest with cross-cutting analysis. Use when the user says 'search slack for', 'what did we discuss about', 'slack context for', or 'what does the team think about'. Differs from slack:find-discussions, which returns raw message results without synthesis.
- compound-engineering:ce-strategy: Create or maintain STRATEGY.md - the product's target problem, approach, users, key metrics, and tracks of work. Use when starting a new product, updating direction, or when prompts like 'write our strategy', 'update the roadmap', 'what are we working on', or 'set up the strategy doc' come up. Also triggers when ce-ideate, ce-brainstorm, or ce-plan need upstream grounding and no strategy doc exists yet.
- compound-engineering:ce-test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:ce-worktree: Create an isolated git worktree for parallel feature work or PR review. Use when starting work that should not disturb the current checkout, or when `ce-work` or `ce-code-review` offers a worktree option.
- compound-engineering:lfg: Run the full autonomous engineering pipeline end-to-end (plan, work, code review, test, commit, push, open PR, watch CI, fix CI failures until green). Use only when the user explicitly requests hands-off execution of a software task and provides a feature description; do not auto-route casual conversation here.
- coding-tutor:coding-tutor: Personalized coding tutorials that build on your existing knowledge and use your actual codebase for examples. Creates a persistent learning trail that compounds over time using the power of AI, spaced repetition and quizes.
- kb:extract: Extract wiki-worthy knowledge from unprocessed session manifests. Sessions are sources — extraction runs the normal ingest workflow.
- kb:kb: Persistent memory vault for Claude Code. Teaches ingest, query, and lint workflows for a markdown-based knowledge vault using Obsidian-flavored markdown.
- kb:refine: Systematic vault refinement — find stale pages, weak connections, merge/split candidates, and backlink gaps. Interactive, user-approved.

- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints

- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").

content.text:
The 404 was transient — `raw.githubusercontent.com` returns **200** now, and the repo is public with `install.sh` on `main`. That URL is fronted by a CDN with a ~5-minute cache; the installer merged in #27 minutes before you ran it, so you hit the cache before it propagated. The installer itself is fine.

Now let me read the README to clean it up.

content.content:
1	# agent-sync
2	
3	`agent-sync` is a Go CLI that keeps AI-agent configuration for multiple tools
4	(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI — and more to come) in sync from a single
5	Git-backed manifest. Think of it as `dotfiles` for agents: one canonical
6	source of skills, rules, commands, and MCP servers; native files rendered
7	into each tool's own conventions.
8	
9	> **Status:** pre-alpha. v1 is in active development. Architecture and
10	> rationale live in:
11	>
12	> - Requirements: [`docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md`](docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md)
13	> - Implementation plan: [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
14	>
15	> Module path `github.com/agent-sync/agent-sync` is provisional until the canonical
16	> hosting location is chosen.
17	
18	## Install
19	
20	Download the prebuilt binary for your platform, verify its checksum, and put it
21	on your PATH — one command:
22	
23	```bash
24	curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
25	```
26	
27	Installs to `/usr/local/bin` (falling back to `~/.local/bin` if that isn't
28	writable). Pin a version or change the location with env vars:
29	
30	```bash
31	curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh \
32	  | AGENT_SYNC_VERSION=v0.1.0 AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR="$HOME/bin" sh
33	```
34	
35	Prefer not to pipe to a shell? Download the archive for your OS/arch from the
36	[Releases page](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases), verify it
37	against `checksums.txt`, extract the `agent-sync` binary, and move it onto your
38	PATH. (Windows: grab the `.zip`.) Git must be on your PATH at runtime.
39	
40	## Quickstart
41	
42	New to agent-sync? [`docs/quickstart.md`](docs/quickstart.md) walks through
43	authoring a canonical repo and running your first `init → sync → validate`,
44	with a copyable example under [`examples/canonical/`](examples/canonical/).
45	
46	## What agent-sync does
47	
48	1. **One manifest per workspace.** A `.agent-sync.yaml` pins the workspace to a
49	   canonical Git repo (pinned by commit SHA by default) and declares which
50	   target tools receive configuration.
51	2. **Explicit `sync`.** `agent-sync sync` materializes the pinned content,
52	   compiles it via per-tool adapters, stages the output, and atomically
53	   swaps it into each tool's reserved subdirectory (e.g.
54	   `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/`, `.codex/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`).
55	3. **Safe by default.** Pinning, offline-strict, TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`
56	   pairs, reserved-prefix ownership with a ledger, and atomic swap with
57	   rollback — designed to fail closed.
58	4. **Capability-honest translation.** A tool-agnostic IR (`agents-md`,
59	   `rule`, `skill`, `command`, `plugin-reference`, `mcp-server-entry`) is
60	   translated by each adapter; per-target capability reports make
61	   lossy translations visible.
62	
63	## Supported tools
64	
65	Adapters translate the tool-agnostic IR into each tool's native files. Three
66	adapters ship bundled today; the rest are planned and tracked against the v1

file.content:
# agent-sync

`agent-sync` is a Go CLI that keeps AI-agent configuration for multiple tools
(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI — and more to come) in sync from a single
Git-backed manifest. Think of it as `dotfiles` for agents: one canonical
source of skills, rules, commands, and MCP servers; native files rendered
into each tool's own conventions.

> **Status:** pre-alpha. v1 is in active development. Architecture and
> rationale live in:
>
> - Requirements: [`docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md`](docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md)
> - Implementation plan: [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
>
> Module path `github.com/agent-sync/agent-sync` is provisional until the canonical
> hosting location is chosen.

## Install

Download the prebuilt binary for your platform, verify its checksum, and put it
on your PATH — one command:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
```

Installs to `/usr/local/bin` (falling back to `~/.local/bin` if that isn't
writable). Pin a version or change the location with env vars:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh \
  | AGENT_SYNC_VERSION=v0.1.0 AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR="$HOME/bin" sh
```

Prefer not to pipe to a shell? Download the archive for your OS/arch from the
[Releases page](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases), verify it
against `checksums.txt`, extract the `agent-sync` binary, and move it onto your
PATH. (Windows: grab the `.zip`.) Git must be on your PATH at runtime.

## Quickstart

New to agent-sync? [`docs/quickstart.md`](docs/quickstart.md) walks through
authoring a canonical repo and running your first `init → sync → validate`,
with a copyable example under [`examples/canonical/`](examples/canonical/).

## What agent-sync does

1. **One manifest per workspace.** A `.agent-sync.yaml` pins the workspace to a
   canonical Git repo (pinned by commit SHA by default) and declares which
   target tools receive configuration.
2. **Explicit `sync`.** `agent-sync sync` materializes the pinned content,
   compiles it via per-tool adapters, stages the output, and atomically
   swaps it into each tool's reserved subdirectory (e.g.
   `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/`, `.codex/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`).
3. **Safe by default.** Pinning, offline-strict, TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`
   pairs, reserved-prefix ownership with a ledger, and atomic swap with
   rollback — designed to fail closed.
4. **Capability-honest translation.** A tool-agnostic IR (`agents-md`,
   `rule`, `skill`, `command`, `plugin-reference`, `mcp-server-entry`) is
   translated by each adapter; per-target capability reports make
   lossy translations visible.

## Supported tools

Adapters translate the tool-agnostic IR into each tool's native files. Three
adapters ship bundled today; the rest are planned and tracked against the v1
roadmap. The `Planned (tier)` label records the intended support tier once the
adapter lands.

| Tool | Status |
|------|--------|
| Claude Code | ✅ Bundled |
| Cursor | ✅ Bundled |
| Codex CLI | ✅ Bundled |
| Pi (`@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`) | Planned (primary) |
| Gemini CLI | Planned (supported) |
| Windsurf | Planned (experimental) |
| LM Studio | Planned (experimental) |

## Requirements

- Go 1.25+
- Git (shelled out for network operations)

## Build

```bash
go build ./cmd/agent-sync
```

## Test

```bash
go test -race ./...
golangci-lint run
```

## Project layout

See [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
for the complete repo layout. The short version:

```
cmd/agent-sync/    CLI entry point
internal/          All implementation packages; no external API yet
docs/              Requirements, plans, specs
```

## Contributing

See [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for guidance when
working on `agent-sync` itself (including from inside a coding agent). These
files are **in-repo agent guidance for contributors**, not output of the
sync pipeline.

## License

Licensed under the [Apache License 2.0](LICENSE). See [`NOTICE`](NOTICE) for
attribution requirements.

content.text:
Found the real problem. This is bigger than the README:

- **There are zero tags and zero releases** (`git tag` empty, `gh release list` empty).
- The `v0.1.0` example in the README → **404** (no such release).

So even though `install.sh` now downloads fine (the 404 you hit was transient CDN), once it runs it tries to fetch a release binary **that doesn't exist**. The installer is documented but non-functional. Let me confirm what the script fetches by default.

content.content:
1	#!/bin/sh
2	# agent-sync installer.
3	#
4	#   curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
5	#
6	# Downloads the prebuilt agent-sync binary for your OS/arch from the latest
7	# GitHub release, verifies its SHA-256 against the release checksums file, and
8	# installs it onto your PATH.
9	#
10	# Environment overrides:
11	#   AGENT_SYNC_VERSION      install a specific tag (e.g. v0.1.0) instead of latest
12	#   AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR  install location (default: /usr/local/bin, falling
13	#                           back to $HOME/.local/bin when that is not writable).
14	#                           When set explicitly, the install fails rather than
15	#                           falling back, so scripted installs get the binary in
16	#                           the exact location or a clear error.
17	#
18	# POSIX sh, no bash-isms. Requires: curl or wget, tar, and sha256sum or shasum.
19	# Verification is mandatory: the installer refuses to install if the release
20	# has no checksums.txt or no SHA-256 tool is available.
21	set -eu
22	
23	REPO="JustinBeaudry/agent-sync"
24	BIN="agent-sync"
25	
26	err() { printf 'install: %s\n' "$1" >&2; exit 1; }
27	info() { printf '%s\n' "$1" >&2; }
28	
29	# --- detect os/arch, mapped to goreleaser's naming ---
30	os=$(uname -s)
31	case "$os" in
32	  Linux) os=linux ;;
33	  Darwin) os=darwin ;;
34	  *) err "unsupported OS '$os'. Windows users: download the .zip from https://github.com/$REPO/releases" ;;
35	esac
36	
37	arch=$(uname -m)
38	case "$arch" in
39	  x86_64 | amd64) arch=amd64 ;;
40	  aarch64 | arm64) arch=arm64 ;;
41	  *) err "unsupported architecture '$arch' (need amd64 or arm64)" ;;
42	esac
43	
44	# --- pick a download tool ---
45	if command -v curl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
46	  dl() { curl -fsSL "$1"; }
47	  dlo() { curl -fsSL -o "$2" "$1"; }
48	elif command -v wget >/dev/null 2>&1; then
49	  dl() { wget -qO- "$1"; }
50	  dlo() { wget -qO "$2" "$1"; }
51	else
52	  err "need curl or wget installed"
53	fi
54	
55	# --- resolve the release and its asset URL ---
56	# Match the asset by the stable parts of goreleaser's name_template
57	# (agent-sync_<version>_<os>_<arch>.tar.gz) rather than reconstructing the
58	# version string, so the installer is robust to the exact tag formatting.
59	if [ -n "${AGENT_SYNC_VERSION:-}" ]; then
60	  api="https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/tags/$AGENT_SYNC_VERSION"
61	else
62	  api="https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/latest"
63	fi
64	
65	release_json=$(dl "$api") || err "could not query the GitHub releases API (rate limited? no release yet?)"
66	
67	# Split the JSON on quotes so each URL/string lands on its own line, then match
68	# with plain POSIX grep (avoids the non-POSIX `grep -o` extension).
69	urls=$(printf '%s' "$release_json" | tr '"' '\n')
70	asset_url=$(printf '%s' "$urls" \
71	  | grep "^https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/.*_${os}_${arch}\.tar\.gz$" \
72	  | head -n1)
73	[ -n "$asset_url" ] || err "no $os/$arch build found in the release. Available assets are listed at https://github.com/$REPO/releases"
74	
75	checksums_url=$(printf '%s' "$urls" \
76	  | grep "^https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/.*checksums\.txt$" \
77	  | head -n1)
78	
79	tag=$(printf '%s' "$release_json" | sed -n 's/.*"tag_name"[ ]*:[ ]*"\([^"]*\)".*/\1/p' | head -n1)
80	archive=$(basename "$asset_url")
81	info "Installing $BIN $tag ($os/$arch)..."
82	
83	# --- download into a temp dir ---
84	tmp=$(mktemp -d 2>/dev/null || mktemp -d -t agent-sync)
85	trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' EXIT INT TERM
86	dlo "$asset_url" "$tmp/$archive" || err "download failed: $asset_url"
87	
88	# --- verify checksum (mandatory: fail closed when it cannot be verified) ---
89	[ -n "$checksums_url" ] || err "release has no checksums.txt — refusing to install unverified"
90	dlo "$checksums_url" "$tmp/checksums.txt" || err "could not download checksums.txt"
91	if command -v sha256sum >/dev/null 2>&1; then
92	  sum=$(sha256sum "$tmp/$archive" | awk '{print $1}')
93	elif command -v shasum >/dev/null 2>&1; then
94	  sum=$(shasum -a 256 "$tmp/$archive" | awk '{print $1}')
95	else
96	  err "no sha256sum or shasum found — cannot verify the download. Install coreutils (sha256sum) or perl (shasum), or download + verify manually from https://github.com/$REPO/releases"
97	fi
98	# -F: the checksums line is matched literally, so dots in the archive name are
99	# not treated as regex wildcards. goreleaser writes "<sha256>  <filename>".
100	grep -Fq "$sum  $archive" "$tmp/checksums.txt" \
101	  || err "checksum mismatch for $archive — refusing to install"
102	info "Checksum verified."
103	
104	# --- extract and install ---
105	tar -xzf "$tmp/$archive" -C "$tmp" "$BIN" || err "could not extract $BIN from $archive"
106	chmod +x "$tmp/$BIN"
107	
108	dir="${AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR:-/usr/local/bin}"
109	
110	if mkdir -p "$dir" 2>/dev/null && mv "$tmp/$BIN" "$dir/$BIN" 2>/dev/null; then
111	  :
112	elif [ -n "${AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR:-}" ]; then
113	  # An explicit target was requested — do not silently install elsewhere.
114	  err "could not install to AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR=$dir (missing or not writable). Create it / fix permissions and retry, or unset it to use the default."
115	elif command -v sudo >/dev/null 2>&1 && sudo install -d "$dir" 2>/dev/null && sudo install -m 0755 "$tmp/$BIN" "$dir/$BIN"; then
116	  # `sudo install` copies as root with an explicit mode, so a non-root user is
117	  # never left owning a binary in a system directory (avoids a local
118	  # privilege-escalation / binary-hijack vector that `sudo mv` from a
119	  # user-owned tmpdir would create on a same-filesystem rename).
120	  :
121	else
122	  dir="$HOME/.local/bin"
123	  if ! { mkdir -p "$dir" && mv "$tmp/$BIN" "$dir/$BIN"; }; then
124	    err "could not install to $dir"
125	  fi
126	  case ":$PATH:" in
127	    *":$dir:"*) ;;
128	    *) info "note: $dir is not on your PATH. Add it: export PATH=\"$dir:\$PATH\"" ;;
129	  esac
130	fi
131	
132	info "Installed: $dir/$BIN"
133	info "Run '$BIN --help' to get started, or see https://github.com/$REPO/blob/main/docs/quickstart.md"

file.content:
#!/bin/sh
# agent-sync installer.
#
#   curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
#
# Downloads the prebuilt agent-sync binary for your OS/arch from the latest
# GitHub release, verifies its SHA-256 against the release checksums file, and
# installs it onto your PATH.
#
# Environment overrides:
#   AGENT_SYNC_VERSION      install a specific tag (e.g. v0.1.0) instead of latest
#   AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR  install location (default: /usr/local/bin, falling
#                           back to $HOME/.local/bin when that is not writable).
#                           When set explicitly, the install fails rather than
#                           falling back, so scripted installs get the binary in
#                           the exact location or a clear error.
#
# POSIX sh, no bash-isms. Requires: curl or wget, tar, and sha256sum or shasum.
# Verification is mandatory: the installer refuses to install if the release
# has no checksums.txt or no SHA-256 tool is available.
set -eu

REPO="JustinBeaudry/agent-sync"
BIN="agent-sync"

err() { printf 'install: %s\n' "$1" >&2; exit 1; }
info() { printf '%s\n' "$1" >&2; }

# --- detect os/arch, mapped to goreleaser's naming ---
os=$(uname -s)
case "$os" in
  Linux) os=linux ;;
  Darwin) os=darwin ;;
  *) err "unsupported OS '$os'. Windows users: download the .zip from https://github.com/$REPO/releases" ;;
esac

arch=$(uname -m)
case "$arch" in
  x86_64 | amd64) arch=amd64 ;;
  aarch64 | arm64) arch=arm64 ;;
  *) err "unsupported architecture '$arch' (need amd64 or arm64)" ;;
esac

# --- pick a download tool ---
if command -v curl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  dl() { curl -fsSL "$1"; }
  dlo() { curl -fsSL -o "$2" "$1"; }
elif command -v wget >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  dl() { wget -qO- "$1"; }
  dlo() { wget -qO "$2" "$1"; }
else
  err "need curl or wget installed"
fi

# --- resolve the release and its asset URL ---
# Match the asset by the stable parts of goreleaser's name_template
# (agent-sync_<version>_<os>_<arch>.tar.gz) rather than reconstructing the
# version string, so the installer is robust to the exact tag formatting.
if [ -n "${AGENT_SYNC_VERSION:-}" ]; then
  api="https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/tags/$AGENT_SYNC_VERSION"
else
  api="https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/releases/latest"
fi

release_json=$(dl "$api") || err "could not query the GitHub releases API (rate limited? no release yet?)"

# Split the JSON on quotes so each URL/string lands on its own line, then match
# with plain POSIX grep (avoids the non-POSIX `grep -o` extension).
urls=$(printf '%s' "$release_json" | tr '"' '\n')
asset_url=$(printf '%s' "$urls" \
  | grep "^https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/.*_${os}_${arch}\.tar\.gz$" \
  | head -n1)
[ -n "$asset_url" ] || err "no $os/$arch build found in the release. Available assets are listed at https://github.com/$REPO/releases"

checksums_url=$(printf '%s' "$urls" \
  | grep "^https://github.com/$REPO/releases/download/.*checksums\.txt$" \
  | head -n1)

tag=$(printf '%s' "$release_json" | sed -n 's/.*"tag_name"[ ]*:[ ]*"\([^"]*\)".*/\1/p' | head -n1)
archive=$(basename "$asset_url")
info "Installing $BIN $tag ($os/$arch)..."

# --- download into a temp dir ---
tmp=$(mktemp -d 2>/dev/null || mktemp -d -t agent-sync)
trap 'rm -rf "$tmp"' EXIT INT TERM
dlo "$asset_url" "$tmp/$archive" || err "download failed: $asset_url"

# --- verify checksum (mandatory: fail closed when it cannot be verified) ---
[ -n "$checksums_url" ] || err "release has no checksums.txt — refusing to install unverified"
dlo "$checksums_url" "$tmp/checksums.txt" || err "could not download checksums.txt"
if command -v sha256sum >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  sum=$(sha256sum "$tmp/$archive" | awk '{print $1}')
elif command -v shasum >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  sum=$(shasum -a 256 "$tmp/$archive" | awk '{print $1}')
else
  err "no sha256sum or shasum found — cannot verify the download. Install coreutils (sha256sum) or perl (shasum), or download + verify manually from https://github.com/$REPO/releases"
fi
# -F: the checksums line is matched literally, so dots in the archive name are
# not treated as regex wildcards. goreleaser writes "<sha256>  <filename>".
grep -Fq "$sum  $archive" "$tmp/checksums.txt" \
  || err "checksum mismatch for $archive — refusing to install"
info "Checksum verified."

# --- extract and install ---
tar -xzf "$tmp/$archive" -C "$tmp" "$BIN" || err "could not extract $BIN from $archive"
chmod +x "$tmp/$BIN"

dir="${AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR:-/usr/local/bin}"

if mkdir -p "$dir" 2>/dev/null && mv "$tmp/$BIN" "$dir/$BIN" 2>/dev/null; then
  :
elif [ -n "${AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR:-}" ]; then
  # An explicit target was requested — do not silently install elsewhere.
  err "could not install to AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR=$dir (missing or not writable). Create it / fix permissions and retry, or unset it to use the default."
elif command -v sudo >/dev/null 2>&1 && sudo install -d "$dir" 2>/dev/null && sudo install -m 0755 "$tmp/$BIN" "$dir/$BIN"; then
  # `sudo install` copies as root with an explicit mode, so a non-root user is
  # never left owning a binary in a system directory (avoids a local
  # privilege-escalation / binary-hijack vector that `sudo mv` from a
  # user-owned tmpdir would create on a same-filesystem rename).
  :
else
  dir="$HOME/.local/bin"
  if ! { mkdir -p "$dir" && mv "$tmp/$BIN" "$dir/$BIN"; }; then
    err "could not install to $dir"
  fi
  case ":$PATH:" in
    *":$dir:"*) ;;
    *) info "note: $dir is not on your PATH. Add it: export PATH=\"$dir:\$PATH\"" ;;
  esac
fi

info "Installed: $dir/$BIN"
info "Run '$BIN --help' to get started, or see https://github.com/$REPO/blob/main/docs/quickstart.md"

content.text:
The installer script is solid and fail-closed — but here's the actual root cause of "did you even test this":

**There are no releases.** Run today, `curl … | sh` downloads `install.sh` (now 200), then hits `releases/latest` → GitHub 404s a repo with zero releases → the script dies at `"could not query the GitHub releases API (… no release yet?)"`. The README documents an install path that **cannot work until a release is published**. Cleaning up the README alone won't fix what you hit.

Let me check how a release gets cut before I recommend the fix.

4	  push:

17	        uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	        with:
19	          fetch-depth: 0
20	
21	      - name: Set up Go
22	        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
23	        with:
24	          go-version: "1.25.x"
25	          check-latest: true
26	          cache: true
27

29	        run: |
30	          sudo apt-get update
31	          sudo apt-get install -y minisign
32	
33	      - name: Run GoReleaser
34	        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
35	        with:
36	          version: "~> v2"
37	          args: release --clean
38	        env:
39	          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
40	          # Optional release-signing secret: contents of an UNENCRYPTED minisign
41	          # secret key (`minisign -G -W`). When unset, goreleaser still publishes
42	          # SHA-256 checksums; the signature is skipped (see .goreleaser.yaml).
43	          MINISIGN_KEY: ${{ secrets.MINISIGN_KEY }}

file.content:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  goreleaser:
    name: goreleaser
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Set up Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: "1.25.x"
          check-latest: true
          cache: true

      - name: Install minisign
        run: |
          sudo apt-get update
          sudo apt-get install -y minisign

      - name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: "~> v2"
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          # Optional release-signing secret: contents of an UNENCRYPTED minisign
          # secret key (`minisign -G -W`). When unset, goreleaser still publishes
          # SHA-256 checksums; the signature is skipped (see .goreleaser.yaml).
          MINISIGN_KEY: ${{ secrets.MINISIGN_KEY }}

content.text:
Here's the picture. The script and `.goreleaser.yaml` are fine — pushing a `v*` tag triggers `release.yml`, which runs goreleaser and publishes binaries + `checksums.txt`. **No tag has ever been pushed**, so there's no release, so `curl … | sh` dies after downloading the script. That's what you hit (the transient raw-CDN 404 just masked it).

So "clean up the README and push to main" fixes the cosmetics but leaves `curl | sh` broken. The thing that actually makes it work is cutting a release. That's an outward-facing publish decision, so I want your call before I tag anything.
