toolUseResult.originalFile:
# agent-sync

`agent-sync` is a Go CLI that keeps AI-agent configuration for multiple tools
(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI — and more to come) in sync from a single
Git-backed manifest. Think of it as `dotfiles` for agents: one canonical
source of skills, rules, commands, and MCP servers; native files rendered
into each tool's own conventions.

> **Status:** pre-alpha. v1 is in active development. Architecture and
> rationale live in:
>
> - Requirements: [`docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md`](docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md)
> - Implementation plan: [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
>
> Module path `github.com/agent-sync/agent-sync` is provisional until the canonical
> hosting location is chosen.

## Install

Download the prebuilt binary for your platform, verify its checksum, and put it
on your PATH — one command:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
```

Installs to `/usr/local/bin` (falling back to `~/.local/bin` if that isn't
writable). Pin a version or change the location with env vars:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh \
  | AGENT_SYNC_VERSION=v0.1.0 AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR="$HOME/bin" sh
```

Prefer not to pipe to a shell? Download the archive for your OS/arch from the
[Releases page](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases), verify it
against `checksums.txt`, extract the `agent-sync` binary, and move it onto your
PATH. (Windows: grab the `.zip`.) Git must be on your PATH at runtime.

## Quickstart

New to agent-sync? [`docs/quickstart.md`](docs/quickstart.md) walks through
authoring a canonical repo and running your first `init → sync → validate`,
with a copyable example under [`examples/canonical/`](examples/canonical/).

## What agent-sync does

1. **One manifest per workspace.** A `.agent-sync.yaml` pins the workspace to a
   canonical Git repo (pinned by commit SHA by default) and declares which
   target tools receive configuration.
2. **Explicit `sync`.** `agent-sync sync` materializes the pinned content,
   compiles it via per-tool adapters, stages the output, and atomically
   swaps it into each tool's reserved subdirectory (e.g.
   `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/`, `.codex/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`).
3. **Safe by default.** Pinning, offline-strict, TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`
   pairs, reserved-prefix ownership with a ledger, and atomic swap with
   rollback — designed to fail closed.
4. **Capability-honest translation.** A tool-agnostic IR (`agents-md`,
   `rule`, `skill`, `command`, `plugin-reference`, `mcp-server-entry`) is
   translated by each adapter; per-target capability reports make
   lossy translations visible.

## Supported tools

Adapters translate the tool-agnostic IR into each tool's native files. Three
adapters ship bundled today; the rest are planned and tracked against the v1
roadmap. The `Planned (tier)` label records the intended support tier once the
adapter lands.

| Tool | Status |
|------|--------|
| Claude Code | ✅ Bundled |
| Cursor | ✅ Bundled |
| Codex CLI | ✅ Bundled |
| Pi (`@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`) | Planned (primary) |
| Gemini CLI | Planned (supported) |
| Windsurf | Planned (experimental) |
| LM Studio | Planned (experimental) |

## Requirements

- Go 1.25+
- Git (shelled out for network operations)

## Build

```bash
go build ./cmd/agent-sync
```

## Test

```bash
go test -race ./...
golangci-lint run
```

## Project layout

See [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
for the complete repo layout. The short version:

```
cmd/agent-sync/    CLI entry point
internal/          All implementation packages; no external API yet
docs/              Requirements, plans, specs
```

## Contributing

See [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for guidance when
working on `agent-sync` itself (including from inside a coding agent). These
files are **in-repo agent guidance for contributors**, not output of the
sync pipeline.

## License

Licensed under the [Apache License 2.0](LICENSE). See [`NOTICE`](NOTICE) for
attribution requirements.

file.content:
# goreleaser configuration for agent-sync.
# Docs: https://goreleaser.com
#
# Local dry run:   goreleaser release --snapshot --clean --skip=sign
# Real release:    pushing a tag matching v* triggers .github/workflows/release.yml
version: 2

project_name: agent-sync

before:
  hooks:
    - go mod tidy
    - go mod verify

builds:
  - id: agent-sync
    main: ./cmd/agent-sync
    binary: agent-sync
    env:
      - CGO_ENABLED=0
    flags:
      - -trimpath
    ldflags:
      - -s -w -X main.version={{ .Version }}
    goos:
      - linux
      - darwin
      - windows
    goarch:
      - amd64
      - arm64
    ignore:
      # windows/arm64 deferred per the v1 plan (Unit 21).
      - goos: windows
        goarch: arm64

archives:
  - id: agent-sync
    formats: [tar.gz]
    format_overrides:
      - goos: windows
        formats: [zip]
    name_template: >-
      {{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}
    files:
      - LICENSE
      - NOTICE
      - README.md
      - CHANGELOG.md

checksum:
  name_template: "checksums.txt"
  algorithm: sha256

# minisign signature over the checksums file, delegated to a shellcheck-able
# script. Activated only when a signing key is present (MINISIGN_KEY), so local
# snapshot builds and forks without the secret do not fail — see
# scripts/sign-release.sh for the key requirements and verification command.
signs:
  - id: minisign
    cmd: scripts/sign-release.sh
    args:
      - "${artifact}"
      - "${signature}"
    artifacts: checksum
    signature: "${artifact}.minisig"

snapshot:
  version_template: "{{ incpatch .Version }}-next"

changelog:
  use: github
  sort: asc
  filters:
    exclude:
      - "^docs:"
      - "^test:"
      - "^chore:"
      - "merge conflict"
      - "Merge pull request"
      - "Merge branch"

release:
  # Auto-publish on tag push so `install.sh` (which resolves the latest
  # published release via the GitHub API) has artifacts to fetch. Flip back to
  # `draft: true` if you want a manual review gate before each release goes live.
  draft: false
  prerelease: auto
  footer: |
    ---
    SHA-256 checksums are in `checksums.txt`. When present, `checksums.txt.minisig`
    is a minisign signature over that file. See the threat model
    (`docs/threat-model.md`) for the verification procedure.

content.text: Archive naming and binary name match `install.sh` (goreleaser strips the `v`, but the script greps only on `_os_arch.tar.gz`, so that's fine). But the archive bundles `CHANGELOG.md` and signing shells out to `scripts/sign-release.sh` — if either is missing, the release job fails. Let me confirm they exist.

1	# Changelog
2	
3	All notable changes to `agent-sync` are documented in this file.
4	
5	The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
6	and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).
7	
8	While the project is pre-1.0, minor version bumps may include breaking changes;
9	the adapter wire protocol follows its own "freeze the frame, grow capabilities"

# Changelog

All notable changes to `agent-sync` are documented in this file.

The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

While the project is pre-1.0, minor version bumps may include breaking changes;
the adapter wire protocol follows its own "freeze the frame, grow capabilities"
compatibility policy documented in `docs/spec/adapter-protocol-v1.md`.

## [Unreleased]

### Added

- Apache-2.0 `LICENSE` and `NOTICE` files; license declared in `README`.
- `docs/threat-model.md` — supply-chain and filesystem-safety threat model.
- `.goreleaser.yaml` and `.github/workflows/release.yml` — reproducible
  multi-platform release packaging (darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64,
  linux/arm64, windows/amd64) with SHA-256 checksums and optional minisign
  signatures.
- CI coverage gate: total statement coverage is enforced against the 80% floor
  mandated by CLAUDE.md and fails the build on a drop. Added behavioral tests
  for previously-untested packages (`internal/validate`, `conformance/echo`,
  `cmd/agent-sync`) plus the adapter op round-trip, watch sync, trust store,
  and conformance assertion paths to clear it.
- Fixed a nil-pointer panic in `adapterkit.ExitError.Error()` (the `e == nil`
  guard dereferenced `e.Code` in the same branch), surfaced by the new tests.
- CI now fails loud if the real-git/real-filesystem end-to-end tests are
  skipped, via `AGENT_SYNC_REQUIRE_GIT=1`.

### Changed

- `README` tool-support table now distinguishes bundled adapters (Claude Code,
  Cursor) from planned ones, instead of advertising unimplemented tiers.

### Removed

- Roo Code from the planned tool list (decommissioned upstream).

content.text:
Real bug this time, not transient. Root cause: goreleaser's `signs` block declares `checksums.txt.minisig` as a publishable artifact, but `MINISIGN_KEY` isn't configured, so `sign-release.sh` no-ops without producing the file — then goreleaser fails trying to upload a signature that was never created. The "optional signing" design is broken: goreleaser doesn't know the signature was conditionally skipped.

`install.sh` only verifies `checksums.txt`, not the signature — so the right fix is to genuinely skip signing when no key is present (and auto-enable it when a key is added later). Let me check what the partial run left behind, then fix.

input.command: echo "=== did a partial release get created? ===" && gh release view v0.1.0 2>&1 | head -20; echo "=== assets ===" && gh release view v0.1.0 --json assets --jq '.assets[].name' 2>&1

content.text:
The partial release is a **draft** (goreleaser uploads assets, then publishes last — the minisig upload failed before publish). Good news: all 6 real assets (binaries + `checksums.txt`) built and uploaded fine. Only signing + final publish failed. Confirms the fix.

Let me make signing genuinely optional in the workflow:

input.old_string:
- name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: "~> v2"
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          # Optional release-signing secret: contents of an UNENCRYPTED minisign
          # secret key (`minisign -G -W`). When unset, goreleaser still publishes
          # SHA-256 checksums; the signature is skipped (see .goreleaser.yaml).
          MINISIGN_KEY: ${{ secrets.MINISIGN_KEY }}
input.new_string:
- name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: "~> v2"
          # Signing is opt-in: goreleaser registers checksums.txt.minisig as a
          # publishable artifact whenever the `signs` block runs, so without a
          # key the no-op sign script leaves nothing to upload and the release
          # fails. Skip the whole sign stage unless MINISIGN_KEY is configured;
          # checksums.txt (which install.sh verifies against) ships either way.
          args: ${{ secrets.MINISIGN_KEY != '' && 'release --clean' || 'release --clean --skip=sign' }}
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          # Optional release-signing secret: contents of an UNENCRYPTED minisign
          # secret key (`minisign -G -W`). Set it and signing turns on automatically.
          MINISIGN_KEY: ${{ secrets.MINISIGN_KEY }}

          # Signing is opt-in: goreleaser registers checksums.txt.minisig as a

toolUseResult.originalFile:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  goreleaser:
    name: goreleaser
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Set up Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: "1.25.x"
          check-latest: true
          cache: true

      - name: Install minisign
        run: |
          sudo apt-get update
          sudo apt-get install -y minisign

      - name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: "~> v2"
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

content.text:
Done — and verified end-to-end. `curl … | sh` now resolves the latest release, verifies the checksum, and installs a working binary.

## What was actually wrong

The 404 you hit was a red herring on top of a real bug:

1. **Transient CDN miss** — `raw.githubusercontent.com` is CDN-fronted (~5 min cache). `install.sh` merged in #27 minutes before you ran it, so you fetched it before the edge cached it. It returns 200 now.
2. **The real bug: there was no release.** Zero tags, zero releases. Even once the script downloaded, it queried `releases/latest` → GitHub 404 → died. The README advertised an installer that *could not work*. So: yes, this wasn't end-to-end tested.
3. **And the release pipeline itself was broken.** Tagging v0.1.0 ran goreleaser, which failed publishing `checksums.txt.minisig` — the `signs` block registers the signature as a publishable artifact, but with no `MINISIGN_KEY` the sign script no-ops and never creates the file. "Optional signing" was never actually optional.

## What I did (all on `main`)

- **`fix(release)`** (e2ea7b3) — skip the sign stage unless `MINISIGN_KEY` is set; `checksums.txt` (the only thing `install.sh` verifies) ships either way. Adding the key later auto-re-enables signing.
- **`docs`** (476cdaf) — trimmed README clutter (provisional module-path note, wordy intro); stamped CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` → `[0.1.0] - 2026-06-16`; noted Codex CLI as bundled.
- **Cut v0.1.0** — release published (not draft), all 6 assets + `checksums.txt` present.
- **Verified the exact command you ran**: `Installing agent-sync v0.1.0 (darwin/arm64)… Checksum verified… Installed`. ✅

## One cosmetic follow-up (didn't touch it)

The released binary reports `version unknown (built from source)` and has no `version` subcommand, despite goreleaser injecting `-X main.version`. So the install works, but `agent-sync --version` won't say `v0.1.0`. The ldflag target (`main.version`) likely isn't what the version command reads. Want me to wire that up? It's a one-liner-ish fix but separate from what broke your install.

55	
56	// RootDeps injects process-level dependencies. Zero values resolve to the
57	// real process streams and clock, so production passes RootDeps{} and
58	// tests inject buffers.
59	type RootDeps struct {
60		In      io.Reader
61		Out     io.Writer
62		Err     io.Writer
63		Now     func() time.Time
64		Version string
65	}
66	
67	func (d RootDeps) in() io.Reader {
68		if d.In != nil {
69			return d.In
70		}
71		return os.Stdin
72	}
73	
74	func (d RootDeps) out() io.Writer {
75		if d.Out != nil {
76			return d.Out
77		}
78		return os.Stdout
79	}
80	
81	func (d RootDeps) err() io.Writer {
82		if d.Err != nil {
83			return d.Err
84		}
85		return os.Stderr
86	}
87	
88	func (d RootDeps) now() func() time.Time {
89		if d.Now != nil {
90			return d.Now
91		}
92		return time.Now
93	}
94	
95	// NewRootCommand builds the `agent-sync` root command with persistent flags
96	// and every subcommand mounted. It mirrors the existing factory+Deps
97	// pattern (NewTrustCommand/NewAdapterCommand). The returned command is
98	// fully functional via Execute() without Fang; Fang styling is applied at
99	// the main boundary (KTD-8) once the TUI dependencies land.
100	func NewRootCommand(deps RootDeps) *cobra.Command {
101		flags := &PersistentFlags{}
102	
103		root := &cobra.Command{
104			Use:   "agent-sync",
105			Short: "Keep AI-agent configuration in sync from a Git-backed manifest",
106			Long: "agent-sync syncs AI-agent configuration for multiple tools (Claude Code, " +
107				"Cursor, and more) from a single Git-backed canonical manifest.",
108			Version:       deps.Version,
109			SilenceUsage:  true,
110			SilenceErrors: true,
111			// Resolve the per-invocation Access + logger once and stash them in
112			// the context so every subcommand reads the same resolution (KTD-5,
113			// KTD-6). cobra runs the root's PersistentPreRunE before the
114			// subcommand's RunE.
115			PersistentPreRunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, _ []string) error {
116				access := resolveAccessFromFlags(cmd, deps, flags)
117				rc := &runtimeContext{
118					Access: access,
119					Logger: newLogger(deps.err(), access, flags.LogLevel),
120					Flags:  *flags,
121					Deps:   deps,
122				}
123				cmd.SetContext(context.WithValue(cmd.Context(), runtimeKey{}, rc))
124				return nil
125			},
126		}
127	
128		root.SetIn(deps.in())
129		root.SetOut(deps.out())
130		root.SetErr(deps.err())
131	
132		pf := root.PersistentFlags()
133		pf.StringVar(&flags.Workspace, "workspace", "", "workspace directory (default: discover from cwd)")
134		pf.StringVar(&flags.Output, "output", "", "output format: text or json (default: text on a TTY, json when piped)")
135		pf.StringVar(&flags.LogLevel, "log-level", "info", "log level: debug, info, warn, error")
136		pf.BoolVar(&flags.Offline, "offline", false, "forbid all network operations")
137		pf.BoolVar(&flags.NonInteractive, "non-interactive", false, "never prompt; fail fast on a missing required value")
138		pf.Bool("yes", false, "alias for --non-interactive")
139	
140		// Mount subcommands.
141		root.AddCommand(newInitCommand(deps))
142		root.AddCommand(newSyncCommand(deps))
143		root.AddCommand(newStatusCommand())
144		root.AddCommand(newValidateCommand(deps))

content.text: Root cause fully confirmed: `buildVersion` honors `opts.version` if set, otherwise falls back to that placeholder. The fix is `fang.WithVersion(version)` at the `main.go` boundary. Per the repo's TDD rule, let me write the failing test first. Let me read the existing main test.

The existing tests deliberately avoid fang's styled bytes — but the version *content* is exactly the bug, so I need to assert on it. Let me check how fang emits the version output (and to which writer) so the test captures it correctly.

Got it — cobra prints the version plainly to `root.OutOrStdout()` (fang only styles help/errors), and `run()` wires that to `os.Stdout`. So a stdout-capture test is clean, no ANSI. Let me write the failing test first.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Changelog

All notable changes to `agent-sync` are documented in this file.

The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

While the project is pre-1.0, minor version bumps may include breaking changes;
the adapter wire protocol follows its own "freeze the frame, grow capabilities"
compatibility policy documented in `docs/spec/adapter-protocol-v1.md`.

## [Unreleased]

## [0.1.0] - 2026-06-16

### Added

- Apache-2.0 `LICENSE` and `NOTICE` files; license declared in `README`.
- `docs/threat-model.md` — supply-chain and filesystem-safety threat model.
- `.goreleaser.yaml` and `.github/workflows/release.yml` — reproducible
  multi-platform release packaging (darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64,
  linux/arm64, windows/amd64) with SHA-256 checksums and optional minisign
  signatures.
- CI coverage gate: total statement coverage is enforced against the 80% floor
  mandated by CLAUDE.md and fails the build on a drop. Added behavioral tests
  for previously-untested packages (`internal/validate`, `conformance/echo`,
  `cmd/agent-sync`) plus the adapter op round-trip, watch sync, trust store,
  and conformance assertion paths to clear it.
- Fixed a nil-pointer panic in `adapterkit.ExitError.Error()` (the `e == nil`
  guard dereferenced `e.Code` in the same branch), surfaced by the new tests.
- CI now fails loud if the real-git/real-filesystem end-to-end tests are
  skipped, via `AGENT_SYNC_REQUIRE_GIT=1`.

### Changed

- `README` tool-support table now distinguishes bundled adapters (Claude Code,
  Cursor, Codex CLI) from planned ones, instead of advertising unimplemented tiers.

### Removed

- Roo Code from the planned tool list (decommissioned upstream).

+  placeholder. `fang.Execute` overwrites `root.Version` from build info, so the
+  value is now passed back through `fang.WithVersion`.

content.text:
Done. Pushed to main as `9b6b750`.

## The bug
`fang.Execute()` overwrites cobra's `root.Version` with its own build-info lookup (`fang.go:137-138`), which falls back to `"unknown (built from source)"` when the binary wasn't installed via `go install`. So the goreleaser `-ldflags -X main.version=0.1.0` value was being injected correctly into `main.version` — but fang threw it away before `--version` ever read it.

## The fix
- **`cmd/agent-sync/main.go`** — pass the version back through `fang.WithVersion(version)`, which `buildVersion()` honors over the placeholder.
- **`cmd/agent-sync/main_test.go`** — added `TestRun_VersionOutputUsesInjectedVersion` (TDD: confirmed RED → GREEN). It sets the package `version`, captures stdout, and asserts the injected value appears and the placeholder doesn't — guarding against silent regression.
- **CHANGELOG** — recorded under `[Unreleased]`.

## Verified
- New test fails before the fix, passes after.
- `go vet ./...` clean, `go test -race ./...` all packages pass, `golangci-lint` 0 issues.
- Release-style build proves it end-to-end: `go build -ldflags "-X main.version=v0.1.0-test"` → `agent-sync version v0.1.0-test`.

Note: v0.1.0 is already published with the old binary, so its `--version` still shows the placeholder. The next tag you cut will pick up the fix — no action needed unless you want to re-roll v0.1.0.

input.prompt:
Investigate the agent-sync Go codebase at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync to answer a capability question. I need precise, evidence-backed answers with file:line references.

Questions:
1. Is there any support for a `.agents` folder (or `.agent`, `.agents/`) in a repo? Search for the literal strings ".agents", "agents" as a directory name, and any configurable source/canonical directory naming.
2. What is the manifest file called and what does it configure? (Look for `.agent-sync.yaml` or similar — how does a workspace point at its canonical source? Is the source a remote git repo only, or can it be a local/in-repo directory?)
3. How are "skills" handled? Search for "skill" across the codebase. What IR kinds exist (look for things like `agents-md`, `rule`, `skill`, `command`, `plugin-reference`, `mcp-server-entry`)? How does a skill flow from canonical source to a target tool's output directory?
4. Is there any notion of "per-repo" vs global configuration? Can a repo define its own skills locally, or must everything come from a pinned remote canonical repo? Look at how the canonical source is resolved (git materialization, pinning by SHA, etc.).
5. What directories do adapters write into per tool (e.g. `.claude/`, `.cursor/`, `.codex/`)? Where is the reserved subdirectory naming defined?

Look especially at: the manifest loading code (internal/manifest), the canonical source resolution (internal/git, internal/workspace, internal/engine), the IR kinds (internal/ir), the sync flow (internal/sync), and the bundled adapters (internal/adapter/bundled/*). Also check docs/ (README, AGENTS.md, docs/quickstart.md, docs/plans, docs/spec) for the intended design.

Report concrete findings: what exists today, what's the canonical-source model, how skills are represented and emitted, and whether anything like an in-repo `.agents` source directory is supported. Quote file:line for each claim. Be thorough — search multiple naming conventions.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile my findings:

## Agent-Sync Codebase Investigation — Findings

Based on a thorough investigation of the agent-sync Go codebase, here are precise, evidence-backed answers to your capability questions:

### 1. **Support for `.agents` folder as a canonical source**

**Finding: NO support for `.agents` folder as a canonical source input.**

The codebase supports only **remote Git repositories** and **local filesystem paths** as canonical sources. The canonical source configuration in `.agent-sync.yaml` (line 33-34 of `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/manifest/schema.go`) accepts:
- `url`: A Git repository URL (remote)
- `local_path`: An absolute filesystem path to a cloned repository

There is **no mechanism** to configure a `.agents` directory as the canonical source. The IR decoder (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/ir/decode.go:35-40`) always expects to read from a git repository object keyed by commit SHA. The canonical repo layout is strictly defined at (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/docs/spec/ir-v1.md:42-59`) with subdirectories `rules/`, `skills/`, `commands/`, `mcp/`, and `plugins/` — not `.agents/` as an input source.

### 2. **Manifest file naming and configuration**

**The manifest is `.agent-sync.yaml` (literal, lowercase, singular).**

Located at: `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/workspace/workspace.go:21`
```go
const ManifestName = ".agent-sync.yaml"
```

**What it configures:**
- **Canonical source binding**: Points to exactly one of:
  - A remote Git repo (`canonical.url`)
  - A local filesystem path (`canonical.local_path`)
- **Source pinning**: Pins by Git commit SHA (`canonical.commit`), always 40-char lowercase hex (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/docs/spec/manifest-v1.md:45`)
- **Trust anchor**: `trusted_sha` mirrors `canonical.commit` for CI validation (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/docs/spec/manifest-v1.md:146-151`)
- **Target tools**: `targets: [claude, cursor, codex]` declares which adapters are active
- **Scope hint**: Optional `scope: project|user|global` for emission paths
- **Cache override**: Optional workspace-local cache location
- **Adapter declarations**: Reserved for future use (Unit 20); v1 bundles adapters inline

**Canonical source is always a Git repository**, not an in-repo directory. Both local and remote sources are materialized via the git package (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/git/materialize.go:30-70`) and pinned by commit SHA.

### 3. **IR kinds (Intermediate Representation concepts)**

**Six closed-set IR kinds** defined in `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/ir/types.go:22-29`:

```go
KindAgentsMD        Kind = "agents-md"
KindRule            Kind = "rule"
KindSkill           Kind = "skill"
KindCommand         Kind = "command"
KindPluginReference Kind = "plugin-reference"
KindMCPServerEntry  Kind = "mcp-server-entry"
```

**Skill flow from canonical source to target output:**

1. **Canonical repo layout** (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/docs/spec/ir-v1.md:49-52`):
   - `skills/<id>/SKILL.md` (markdown with optional frontmatter)
   - `skills/<id>/<asset...>` (arbitrary asset files)

2. **IR decoder** extracts nodes and assets (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/ir/decode.go:108-139`):
   - Validates skill ID against pattern `[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-_]{0,63}`
   - Bundles assets via `SkillsByID()` function (line 226)
   - Returns `[]Node` + `map[string]Skill` with Asset lists

3. **Adapter-specific emission** (example Codex):
   - **Output location**: `.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md` (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/codex/emit_reserved.go:18`)
   - **Per-skill directory**: `mkdir(.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>)` (line 41)
   - **Main entry**: `write_file(.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md)` (line 47)
   - **Assets**: `write_file(.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/<asset.RelPath>)` for each asset (lines 56-82)

**Claude adapter** emits to `.claude/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md` (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/claude/capabilities.yaml:26-32`)

**Cursor adapter** does not support skills (unsupported in capabilities)

### 4. **Per-repo vs global configuration model**

**Configuration model: Single canonical source per workspace only.**

- **Pinning is default** (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/AGENTS.md:44-46`):
  - `agent-sync init` resolves refs to SHAs and commits them to `.agent-sync.yaml`
  - Offline-strict mode requires either pinned + cached, or online access
  
- **No per-repo local skills directory support**: Everything comes from the pinned canonical source. A repo cannot define its own `.agents` folder to supplement or override the canonical source — the canonical source is the single source of truth.

- **Trust model** (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/manifest/schema.go:24-26`):
  - `trusted_sha` committed to git as a project-level trust anchor
  - CI fails closed if resolved SHA drifts from `trusted_sha`
  
- **Floating mode (reserved)**: `floating: true` is not yet in v1 schema; planned for Unit 5/6 (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/docs/spec/manifest-v1.md:153-156`)

**All skills/rules/commands must originate from the pinned canonical source.** Local modifications are rejected at the `validate` stage; users cannot define local skills outside the canonical source.

### 5. **Adapter output directory structure**

**Reserved prefixes per tool** (declared in `capabilities.yaml`):

| Tool | Reserved Prefix | Reference |
|------|---|---|
| Claude Code | `.claude` | `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/claude/capabilities.yaml:4` |
| Cursor | `.cursor` | `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/cursor/capabilities.yaml:4` |
| Codex CLI | `.codex` | `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/codex/capabilities.yaml:4` |

**Output locations by kind:**

| IR Kind | Claude | Cursor | Codex |
|---------|--------|--------|-------|
| `agents-md` | `CLAUDE.md` (tool-owned) | AGENTS.md (tool-owned) | `AGENTS.md` (tool-owned, section-merged) |
| `rule` | `.claude/rules/agent-sync/<id>.md` | `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/<id>.mdc` | Unsupported (degradation warning) |
| `skill` | `.claude/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md` + assets | Unsupported | `.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md` + assets |
| `command` | `.claude/commands/agent-sync/<id>.md` | Unsupported | Unsupported |
| `mcp-server-entry` | `.mcp.json` (tool-owned, JSON-pointer merge) | `.cursor/mcp.json` (tool-owned, TOML merge) | `.codex/config.toml` (tool-owned, TOML merge under `[mcp_servers.agentsync_<id>]`) |
| `plugin-reference` | Unsupported | Unsupported | Unsupported |

**Shared subdirectory note**: `.agents/skills` is a **cross-tool convention** for Codex and (future) pi adapters — multiple tools read from the same `.agents/skills` tree. The engine manages only `agent-sync-<id>` leaf entries within it, not the shared parent (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/codex/emit_reserved.go:14-20`).

**Reserved prefix validation** (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/manifest/load.go`): Prevents one adapter's prefix nesting inside another's; trailing slashes are stripped on load.

---

## Summary

**Today's canonical-source model is git-only** (remote or local filesystem clone). There is **no support for a `.agents` folder as a canonical source input**. The `.agents/skills` directory is only an **adapter output location** (Codex/pi emitted skill tree), not a configuration input.

All configuration flows from a single external-or-local git repository pinned by commit SHA. Per-repo skill authoring is not supported; all skills originate from the canonical source. The manifest `.agent-sync.yaml` declares exactly which canonical source (URL or local path) and commit to use; no workspace-local overrides or extensions are possible.

lastPrompt: Does it support having a .agents folder in a repo, and allowing us to have per-repo skills?

content.text:
Short answer: **no, not in the way you're describing.** `.agents/` exists in agent-sync today only as an *output* location, never as an *input* you author in.

Here's the precise picture:

**`.agents/` is an output convention, not a source.** The Codex adapter writes compiled skills to `.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md` (`internal/adapter/bundled/codex/emit_reserved.go:18`) — it's a cross-tool tree that Codex (and the planned `pi` adapter) read from. The engine only owns the `agent-sync-<id>` leaf entries inside it. There's no code path that *reads* a repo's `.agents/` folder as canonical content.

**The source model is single-canonical, git-only, pinned by SHA.** A workspace's `.agent-sync.yaml` (`internal/workspace/workspace.go:21`) points at exactly one canonical source — either `canonical.url` (remote git) or `canonical.local_path` (a local clone) — pinned to a 40-char commit SHA. Everything materializes from that one repo (`internal/git/materialize.go`). Skills live there under `skills/<id>/SKILL.md` (`docs/spec/ir-v1.md`), get decoded to IR `kind: skill` (`internal/ir/types.go:24`), and are emitted per-tool: `.claude/skills/agent-sync-<id>/` for Claude, `.agents/skills/...` for Codex (Cursor doesn't support skills).

**So "per-repo skills" specifically:** not supported. You can't drop a `.agents/` (or any) folder into a repo and have agent-sync treat it as a local skill source, and you can't overlay local skills on top of the pinned canonical source. `validate` fails closed on anything outside the canonical-derived content. The closest existing lever is pointing `canonical.local_path` at a local git repo — but that's still a separate pinned repo, not in-repo authoring.

---

What you're really asking for is a **local/in-repo skill source** — either as the canonical source itself or as an overlay on top of a remote one. That's a real feature, not a config flag that exists. It'd need: a manifest source-kind for an in-repo directory (bypassing git materialization/SHA-pinning, or pinning by content hash), reserved-prefix/ownership handling so it doesn't collide with the canonical output, and a precedence rule if it overlays a remote canonical.

Want me to write that up as a design proposal in `docs/` (and check the plan docs for whether an overlay/floating mode is already scoped — there's a reserved `floating: true` and an "Unit 5/6" reference I'd want to confirm)?

message.content:
<command-message>compound-engineering:lfg</command-message>
<command-name>/compound-engineering:lfg</command-name>
<command-args>Yes, and if we existing work around that, lets get that ready to get done and get it to done. This is whats necessary to ship.</command-args>

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/justinbeaudry/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.9.3/skills/lfg

CRITICAL: You MUST execute every step below IN ORDER. Do NOT skip any required step. Do NOT jump ahead to coding or implementation. The plan phase (step 1) MUST be completed and verified BEFORE any work begins. Violating this order produces bad output.

When invoking any skill referenced below, resolve its name against the available-skills list the host platform provides and use that exact entry. Some platforms list skills under a plugin namespace (e.g., `compound-engineering:ce-plan`); others list the bare name. Invoking a short-form guess that isn't in the list will fail — always match a listed entry verbatim before calling the Skill/Task tool.

1. Invoke the `ce-plan` skill with `Yes, and if we existing work around that, lets get that ready to get done and get it to done. This is whats necessary to ship.`.

   GATE: STOP. If ce-plan reported the task is non-software and cannot be processed in pipeline mode, stop the pipeline and inform the user that LFG requires software tasks. Otherwise, verify that the `ce-plan` workflow produced a plan file in `docs/plans/`. If no plan file was created, invoke `ce-plan` again with `Yes, and if we existing work around that, lets get that ready to get done and get it to done. This is whats necessary to ship.`. Do NOT proceed to step 2 until a written plan exists. **Record the plan file path** — it will be passed to ce-code-review in step 3.

2. Invoke the `ce-work` skill.

   GATE: STOP. Verify that implementation work was performed - files were created or modified beyond the plan. Do NOT proceed to step 3 if no code changes were made.

3. Invoke the `ce-code-review` skill with `mode:autofix plan:<plan-path-from-step-1>`.

   Pass the plan file path from step 1 so ce-code-review can verify requirements completeness. Read the Residual Actionable Work summary the skill emits.

4. **Persist review autofixes** (REQUIRED after step 3, before residual handoff)

   Check `git status --short`. If `ce-code-review mode:autofix` changed files, stage only those review-fix files, commit them with `fix(review): apply autofix feedback`, and push the current branch before continuing. If an upstream exists, run `git push`. If no upstream exists, resolve a writable remote dynamically: prefer `origin` when present, otherwise use `git remote` and choose the first configured remote. Then run `git push --set-upstream <remote> HEAD`. Do not proceed to step 5, run browser tests, or output DONE while review autofix edits remain only in the working tree. If no files changed, explicitly note that there were no review autofixes to persist.

5. **Autonomous residual handoff** (only when step 3 reported one or more residual `downstream-resolver` findings; skip when it reported `Residual actionable work: none.`)

   Do not prompt the user. This step embraces the autopilot contract: residuals must become durable before DONE, but the agent never stops to ask.

   1. Load `references/tracker-defer.md` in **non-interactive mode**. Pass the residual actionable findings from step 3's summary (or the run artifact when the summary was truncated).
   2. Collect the structured return: `{ filed: [...], failed: [...], no_sink: [...] }`.
   3. Compose a `## Residual Review Findings` markdown section from the structured return:
      - For each item in `filed`: a bullet with severity, file:line, title, and a link to the tracker ticket URL.
      - For each item in `failed`: a bullet with severity, file:line, title, and the failure reason (e.g., `Defer failed: gh returned 401 — tracker unavailable`).
      - For each item in `no_sink`: a bullet with severity, file:line, and title inlined verbatim so the PR body or fallback file is the durable record.
   4. Detect the current branch's open PR without prompting:

      ```bash
      gh pr view --json number,url,body,state
      ```

   5. If an open PR exists, update it directly with `gh`; do not load any confirmation-driven PR update skill. Append or replace the `## Residual Review Findings` section in the current PR body, write the new body to an OS temp file, then run:

      ```bash
      gh pr edit PR_NUMBER --body-file BODY_FILE
      ```

   6. If no open PR exists, create a tracked fallback file at `docs/residual-review-findings/<branch-or-head-sha>.md` containing the composed section and the source PR-review run context. Stage only that file, commit it with `docs(review): record residual review findings`, and push the current branch. If an upstream exists, run `git push`. If no upstream exists, resolve a writable remote dynamically: prefer `origin` when present, otherwise use `git remote` and choose the first configured remote. Then run `git push --set-upstream <remote> HEAD`. This is the durable no-PR sink. Do not output DONE until either the existing PR body has been updated or this fallback file commit has been pushed. If both paths fail, stop and report the failed commands; do not silently proceed.

   Never block DONE on tracker filing failures once residuals have been durably recorded. A `no_sink` outcome is success only when the findings are present in the PR body or in the pushed fallback file.

6. Invoke the `ce-test-browser` skill with `mode:pipeline`.

7. Invoke the `ce-commit-push-pr` skill.

   This commits any remaining changes, pushes the branch, and opens a pull request. If step 5 already opened a PR (check with `gh pr view --json number,url,state 2>/dev/null`), skip PR creation but still commit and push any uncommitted changes.

8. **CI watch and autofix loop** (only when an open PR exists for the current branch)

   Detect the PR; if none exists or `gh` is unavailable, skip this step entirely and proceed to step 9.

   ```bash
   gh pr view --json number,url,state
   ```

   For up to **3 fix iterations**, repeat:

   1. Wait for CI to complete:

      ```bash
      gh pr checks --watch
      ```

      If the command exits 0, all checks passed. Break out of the loop and proceed to step 9.

      If it exits non-zero, one or more checks failed. Continue to (2).

   2. Identify failing checks and pull their failure logs. Use `gh pr checks --json name,state,conclusion,workflow,link` to enumerate failures, then for each failing check read the run logs:

      ```bash
      gh run view <run-id> --log-failed
      ```

      where `<run-id>` is parsed from the check's details URL or workflow run.

   3. Read the failure logs, identify the root cause, and apply a fix in the working tree. Do NOT weaken, skip, or mock the failing assertion to make it pass — repair the actual issue. If the failure is a flaky test that has no fix path, document that as the residual outcome below rather than retrying without a code change.

   4. Stage only the files you changed, commit, and push:

      ```bash
      git add <changed-files>
      git commit -m "fix(ci): <one-line summary of the failure repaired>"
      git push
      ```

   5. Return to iteration (1) with the next attempt counter.

   GATE: STOP iterating after 3 failed attempts. If CI is still red after 3 fix cycles:

   - Compose a `## CI Failures Unresolved` markdown section listing each remaining failing check, the failure summary, and the run/check URL.
   - Append or replace this section in the PR body, write the new body to an OS temp file, then run:

     ```bash
     gh pr edit PR_NUMBER --body-file BODY_FILE
     ```

   - Do NOT continue looping. The autopilot contract is "make residuals durable, then exit." Proceed to step 9.

9. Output `<promise>DONE</promise>` when complete

Start with step 1 now. Remember: plan FIRST, then work. Never skip the plan.
Starting the LFG pipeline. Step 1: planning. First let me confirm the existing scope this builds on (the `floating`/Unit 5-6 references I flagged), then invoke `ce-plan`.

content.text: Context confirmed — there's an existing `--floating`/`local-path` source concept but nothing for in-repo `.agents` authoring. Now invoking `ce-plan` (LFG step 1).

input.args:
Yes, and if we existing work around that, lets get that ready to get done and get it to done. This is whats necessary to ship.

CONTEXT: The feature is in-repo / local per-repo skills for agent-sync. Today the source model is single-canonical, git-only, pinned by SHA (.agent-sync.yaml -> canonical.url or canonical.local_path, pinned 40-char commit). Skills only flow from that one pinned repo; a repo cannot author its own skills locally. `.agents/` exists only as a Codex/pi OUTPUT tree (internal/adapter/bundled/codex/emit_reserved.go:18), never as an input source. The user wants to drop per-repo skills into the repo (likely a `.agents/` source folder) and have agent-sync manage them — either as the canonical source or as a local overlay on top of a remote canonical. Existing partial scope to reconcile: a reserved `--floating` flag and `canonical.local_path` already exist (see docs/plans/2026-06-08-007-feat-cli-tui-sync-engine-plan.md). Plan the in-repo local skill source feature, reconciling with that existing work, and get it ready to ship end-to-end.
